text:
The purpose of this paper is an analysis of the judiciary and procedural issues connected with the abolition of communist symbols in public space in Poland — especially problems connected with the distribution of competences between the government deputies (voivodes) and the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN). It will begin with an explanation of the so-called “Decommunization Act” of 2nd of December 2016. According to this legislation, in order to rename an object or take down its monument in the case when this object was linked with totalitarian systems, the voivode shall give an zastępcze order. Before doing this, however, he has to receive an advisory opinion from the IPN.

Last week, Kaczynski’s judiciary again managed to turn the law on its head – and we have the Constitutional Court and the Supreme Administrative Court (NSA) to thank for it. We previously discussed the work of the Constitutional Court which earlier this year ruled that the Institute of National Remembrance’s (IPN) assessment of communist crimes in history textbooks is advisory. The voivode, after receiving IPN’s assessment, has the discretion to either accept or reject the evaluation, primarily on a political basis, depending on the political sympathies of the voivode. As if confirming our fears, the Supreme Administrative Court recently issued a judgement which indicates that the NSA has reversed the principle we were concerned about: IPN’s opinions are non-binding. The NSA has ruled that the opnions can be used as a form of expert testimony and are subject to administrative verification regarding issues such as their methodology, or whether they present a coherent or transparent description of historical events. At first glance this does seem to increase the role of the voivode and diminish the role of IPN — albeit it seems to function as a delaying tactic. This means that the NSA has in principle only restricted the Voivode to verification of the methodology of IPN’s reports. In practice however the verdict is much more complex: The NSA also ruled that if IPN’s reports contain factual descriptions or evaluative historical interpretations that question the nature of communist crimes they must be verified by the administrative authorities to confirm that these are correct statements. The courts will then “verify” whether the Voivode and administrative authorities did this correctly by going into the substance of historical debates about whether a given number of victims exists, about whether the communists carried out a genocide of the Polish nation, or whether Marshal Gikow, or the liquidation of the Warsaw Uprising were really communist crimes and whether or not something or someone can be classified as a symbol of communism in the historical consciousness of the Polish Nation. This decision illustrates perfectly the reach of the ideology of communist crimes in the Polish judiciary and politics and highlights another absurd element of the country’s post-1989 attempts to define its past.

The study offers a critical assessment of this development, arguing that the NSA’s interpretation contradicts the literal wording of the Decommunization Act, which uses the term "confirms" (potwierdza) to describe the IPN’s role, implying a decisive authority. Furthermore, the author highlights the high level of systemic irrationality in requiring voivodes—who lack specialized historical mandates—to verify the findings of a dedicated national research institution. The paper concludes that while IPN opinions must meet high standards of quality, they should be viewed as authoritative statements of state knowledge within a cooperative administrative framework rather than as standard evidence requiring independent historical validation by regional authorities.
